translation: The son was holding the hands of his father.
explanation: manūs is accusative plural of manus (4th declension) — the direct object. The genitive singular also ends -ūs, but here 'patris' is already the genitive; this form is accusative plural.

translation: The gladiators were waging the fight with their hands.
explanation: manibus is the ablative plural of manus (4th declension). Ablative of means: 'with their hands'.

translation: The general gave his hand to the soldier.
explanation: manum is accusative singular of manus — the direct object of dedit (gave).

translation: The poet was telling the story.
explanation: rem is accusative singular of rēs (5th declension) — the direct object of nārrābat.

translation: The students did not know the name of the thing.
explanation: reī is genitive singular of rēs (5th declension) — 'of the thing'. The 5th declension genitive singular ends in -eī.

translation: We saw many things in the forum.
explanation: rēs here is accusative plural (matching multās, feminine accusative plural adjective). In the 5th declension, accusative plural = rēs.

translation: He stood at the hand of the teacher.
explanation: manū is ablative singular of manus (4th declension). The preposition ā/ab takes the ablative. Ablative singular of 4th declension ends in -ū.

translation: The old man was thinking about the matter.
explanation: rē is ablative singular of rēs (5th declension). The preposition dē takes the ablative. Ablative singular of 5th declension ends in -ē.
The genitive singular of 4th declension nouns ends in -ūs (e.g. manūs = of the hand). Do not confuse with the nominative plural, which is also -ūs.
